frameworks.name: serial dependence in working memory
frameworks.one_line: influence of previous stimuli on current working memory contents

frameworks.name: biophysical prefrontal microcircuit model
frameworks.one_line: NMDAR-dependent attractor circuit simulating delayed-response working memory

frameworks.name: short-term synaptic potentiation
frameworks.one_line: NMDAR-mediated longer-timescale plasticity proposed to carry trial-to-trial bias

frameworks.name: cortical excitation-inhibition imbalance
frameworks.one_line: within-trial E/I changes alone cannot explain reduced serial dependence

core_question: Can NMDAR hypofunction in schizophrenia and anti-NMDAR encephalitis explain shared working memory alterations through a common synaptic mechanism?
key_assumptions: schizophrenia and anti-NMDAR encephalitis share NMDAR hypofunction
delayed-response task isolates working memory contents
serial dependence indexes a longer-timescale memory mechanism
microcircuit model captures relevant prefrontal dynamics
manipulations: patient group (schizophrenia, anti-NMDAR encephalitis) vs controls
NMDAR-dependent synaptic parameters in microcircuit model
within-trial excitation vs longer-timescale potentiation in simulations
key_findings: both patient groups show markedly reduced influence of previous stimuli on working memory
memory precision within trials is preserved in both patient groups
within-trial excitation changes destabilize maintenance but do not explain disrupted serial dependence
quantitative model-data fit supports alteration of an NMDAR-dependent longer-timescale mechanism
interpretations: reduced serial dependence reflects deficits in short-term potentiation rather than within-trial dynamics
schizophrenia and anti-NMDAR encephalitis share an NMDAR-dependent synaptic alteration operating across trials
